claims.plain: The Navier-Stokes Millennium Problem remains officially unsolved.
claims.formal: Clay Mathematics Institute lists the Navier-Stokes Equation as Unsolved.
claims.conclusion: No generally accepted proof of Clay alternative A, B, C, or D is recognized.
claims.audit: Current-status claim; recheck before time-sensitive reporting.

claims.plain: Proving any one of four official alternatives resolves the stated problem.
claims.formal: Fefferman's statement requests a proof of A, B, C, or D.
claims.conclusion: Either a global-smoothness theorem or an admissible breakdown construction can resolve the problem.
claims.audit: Narrative paraphrases must defer to the official quantifiers.

claims.plain: Velocity L3 and vorticity L(3/2) are invariant under Navier-Stokes scaling.
claims.formal: For u_lambda=lambda u(lambda x,lambda^2 t), the Lq exponent is 1-3/q; for omega_lambda=lambda^2 omega(lambda x,lambda^2 t), it is 2-3/q.
assumptions: The whole-space scaling is applied without boundary effects.
claims.conclusion: q=3 for velocity and q=3/2 for vorticity are spatially critical.

claims.plain: The unforced smooth equation dissipates kinetic energy.
claims.formal: One half d/dt ||u||_2^2 plus nu ||grad u||_2^2 equals zero.
claims.conclusion: Kinetic energy cannot increase in the smooth unforced evolution.
claims.audit: Energy control alone is not a regularity theorem.

claims.plain: Global finite-energy weak solutions exist in three dimensions.
claims.formal: Leray's construction produces global weak solutions with energy control for finite-energy divergence-free data.
claims.conclusion: Weak global existence holds, without proving global smoothness.
claims.audit: Do not upgrade weak existence to classical existence.

claims.plain: The possible singular set of a suitable weak solution is very small in parabolic measure.
claims.formal: The one-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure of the singular set is zero under the CKN hypotheses.
claims.conclusion: The singular set is restricted but not proven empty.
claims.audit: Partial regularity is not global regularity.

claims.plain: Uniform-in-time spatial L3 control of velocity prevents blow-up.
claims.formal: A suitable/Leray-Hopf solution in L-infinity(0,T;L3(R3)) is regular under the theorem's hypotheses.
assumptions: Velocity bounded in L-infinity time and L3 space
claims.conclusion: The solution is regular through the terminal time.
claims.audit: This is strong L3, not weak-L3.

claims.plain: Finite-q critical Lorentz control of velocity is a regularity class.
claims.formal: L-infinity time L(3,q) space with finite q implies regularity in the stated setting.
claims.audit: The q=infinity weak endpoint is excluded from this claim.

claims.plain: Sufficient coherence of vorticity direction in intense regions depletes stretching enough for regularity.
claims.formal: The Constantin-Fefferman geometric hypothesis implies regularity under its precise integrability and coherence assumptions.
claims.audit: The geometry is a hypothesis, not known to be universal.

claims.plain: An important integrable class of backward self-similar singularities is impossible.
claims.formal: The only Leray self-similar profile in the specified L3 class is trivial.
claims.conclusion: That self-similar blow-up class is excluded.
claims.audit: Does not exclude every asymptotic, discrete, or Type-II scenario.

claims.plain: Scaling and energy cancellation do not by themselves force regularity.
claims.formal: An averaged Navier-Stokes bilinear operator preserving key scaling and cancellation properties admits finite-time blow-up.
assumptions: Replace the true nonlinearity by Tao's averaged operator
claims.conclusion: A regularity proof must use finer structure of the true nonlinearity.
claims.audit: This is not blow-up for the true Navier-Stokes equation.

claims.plain: Finite-energy distributional Navier-Stokes solutions can be nonunique in a weak class.
claims.formal: Buckmaster and Vicol construct distinct weak solutions sharing data in their stated finite-energy class.
assumptions: The weak solution class used in the source
claims.conclusion: Uniqueness fails in that weak class.
claims.audit: Do not restate as smooth-data classical blow-up or general Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness.

claims.plain: Forced Leray solutions can be nonunique for the same force and the same zero initial velocity.
claims.formal: Albritton, Brue, and Colombo construct two distinct Leray solutions with identical force and zero initial velocity.
assumptions: The exact force and solution class in the source
claims.conclusion: Uniqueness fails in the constructed forced setting.
claims.audit: Must not be promoted to Clay C without matching Fefferman's smooth force and classical conclusion.

claims.plain: A 2026 preprint claims certified unforced Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness from singular critical initial data.
claims.formal: The source claims infinitely many suitable Leray-Hopf solutions with the same compactly supported singular initial condition and smoothness for positive times.
claims.conclusion: Unforced suitable Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness in the claimed class.
claims.audit: Independent certificate reproduction pending; singular initial data lie outside Clay A-D.

claims.plain: A 2026 preprint conditionally excludes a structured critical point singularity under log-weighted vorticity-direction control; the full proof chain has been independently reconstructed here with explicit repairs.
claims.formal: For a normalized projected mild solution, uniform weak-L(3/2) vorticity, uniform high-level fixed-centre localization, and a global unit direction extension with uniform bmo_(1/|log r|) control on one terminal interval imply that the proposed T-star is not singular.
assumptions: Fixed-centre uniform critical upper envelope or equivalent uniform high-level single-ball localization
Existence of a global unit direction extension with uniform log-weighted BMO control
Projected mild velocity normalization or a uniformly bounded spatially constant harmonic background
claims.conclusion: The proposed first singular time is regular.
claims.audit: O2607-01 through O2607-16 are closed by independent analytic reconstructions. The proof survives after documented repairs to profile and direction quantifiers, weak-Lorentz commutator interpolation, shell endpoints, the terminal interval, slowly varying inversions, velocity normalization, escape-time selection, slit-domain harmonic measure, and the final supremum logic.

claims.plain: Under a scale-critical Morrey Type-I bound, every terminal singular point carries fixed critical L3 mass in a parabolic ball at every sufficiently late time.
claims.formal: For a finite-energy Leray-Hopf solution on R3 whose first blow-up time is T-star, if the scale-critical local L2 Morrey quantity is uniformly bounded and (x-star,T-star) is singular, then fixed gamma>0 and K<infinity give ||u(t)||_L3(B_{K sqrt(T-star-t)}(x-star))>gamma for every sufficiently late t<T-star.
assumptions: The source's scale-critical Morrey Type-I bound
claims.conclusion: Critical L3 concentration is centred at the singular point at every sufficiently late time.
claims.audit: The theorem is every-late-time rather than merely limsup, but it requires the Type-I Morrey bound and does not place an independently selected carrier near the singular centre.

claims.plain: It is open whether every smooth three-dimensional unforced initial state remains smooth globally.
claims.formal: Neither Clay alternative A nor B has an accepted proof.
assumptions: Arbitrary admissible smooth divergence-free initial data
claims.audit: Recheck official status for future snapshots.
